abstract: The separation of dinitrogen (N2) from methane (CH4) is a critical industrial challenge for natural gas purification, rendered difficult by the similar physical properties of the two gases. In this work, we employ a suite of quantum mechanical computational methods, including density functional theory (DFT) and high-level correlated wave function theory, to predict the performance of a novel metal-organic framework, V-MOF-74. Our calculations reveal that the open V(II) sites within the framework provide a unique electronic environment that favors N2 over CH4 through enhanced π back-bonding. While both gases exhibit similar binding characteristics in the experimentally known Fe-MOF-74, the d3 electronic configuration of V(II) allows for significant electron donation into the unoccupied π* orbitals of N2, resulting in a much higher adsorption enthalpy. This selective interaction is further evidenced by significant N–N vibrational frequency shifts and detailed orbital analysis. These findings identify V-MOF-74 as a high-priority synthetic target for achieving efficient, thermodynamics-based dinitrogen separations.
